context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill one person to save five. I kind of know act consequentialism is relevant, but everything is blurring together, especially the trolley cases. Can you just explain the whole thing simply first?
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s simplify it into 3 parts.

1. What would act consequentialism say?  
Ask yourself: if only outcomes matter, when would killing 1 be allowed?

2. Apply that to the cases.  
For each case, does killing 1 lead to the best overall result, or not?

3. Then criticize it.  
Do your intuitions say act consequentialism handles Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases well—or does it permit too much?

Start with part 1: in one sentence, how would you define act consequentialism in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not give a full explanation or a ready-made solution, because my role is to guide the student to build the argument themselves. Since the student is stuck and asks for a simple overview, I will give only a minimal structure for the assignment, then use targeted Socratic questions that move directly toward a workable thesis. This keeps the help bite-sized, focused on the assignment, and avoids writing the student's answer for them.

exchanges.student: I guess it means an action is morally right if it leads to the best overall consequences compared with the alternatives. I’m not fully sure what counts as “best,” though, and I already feel myself getting lost on how that turns into an answer for killing one to save five.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. For this assignment, “best” usually means something like the outcome with the most overall good or least overall harm.

So test the core case: if the options are really just
A) one dies, or
B) five die,
what would act consequentialism say—and why?

Then the next step is the important one: do you think that same reasoning fits every trolley-style case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid starting definition but is getting lost moving from theory to application. I should affirm the useful part, narrow the focus, and ask one forward-moving question that connects the abstract principle to the assignment's central case. I must avoid supplying a full solution and keep it concise.

exchanges.student: It would say killing one is okay, or even required, because one death is less harm than five deaths. That feels too quick, though, and I’m not sure if I’m supposed to just say that for all the cases or if there’s some bigger principle I’m missing.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the basic act consequentialist move. Don’t stop there.

The assignment asks two separate things:
1. What would act consequentialism say in the cases?
2. Is that the right result?

So now compare cases. Which case seems easiest for act consequentialism, and which seems most troubling? Pick one of Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case and say what AC would predict.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the basic act consequentialist verdict. Now I should help them distinguish between reporting that view and evaluating it across cases, since the assignment explicitly asks both. I will use guided discovery to organize the task into a comparison between theory and intuition, without giving the student's thesis or wording. This advances toward the solution while staying brief and not writing for them.

exchanges.student: I think a trolley case is easiest, because it just looks like numbers: one dies instead of five, so AC says divert it. What’s troubling is that once I say that, I start feeling like AC will also approve a lot of more personal killing cases, and then I’m not sure where I’m supposed to draw the line.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—that tension is the heart of the assignment.

So don’t draw the line yet. First identify what changes.

Between “divert the trolley” and a more personal killing case, what seems morally different to you: intention, means vs side effect, physical involvement, rights, or something else?

Pick the one difference that feels most important and defend it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now at the key philosophical pivot: AC seems plausible in simple trolley switching but overpermissive in more personal or invasive cases. I should help them articulate that tension rather than solve it for them. A focused comparison question will move them toward identifying the morally relevant difference they can later use in a general account, without giving them the theory or wording.

exchanges.student: I think “using someone as a means” feels most important, but I can’t really defend it clearly. I keep sliding back into wanting a big explanation of all the views, because if I just pick one difference I’m worried I’ll miss the actual point of Fuel and Blood too.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re overexpanding. The assignment does not require “all the views”; it requires a comparison and your judgment.

Use this structure:
1. State AC’s rule.
2. Apply it to Fuel, Blood, trolley cases.
3. Say where it seems wrong.
4. Propose the difference that matters.

Now test your idea: in Fuel and Blood, is the one person merely foreseen as harmed, or being used to save the five?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same uncertainty and asking for a broad explanation. Per the rules, I should redirect to the bigger picture, prevent redundancy, and ask them to integrate what we have so far into a tentative written structure. I can provide a minimal roadmap tied to the assignment—act consequentialism, application to cases, and criticism—without explaining all views or giving a solution. A targeted question about Fuel and Blood will move them forward.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, and that’s where I’m getting stuck again. Could you just walk me through Fuel and Blood pretty fully, because I feel like if I only answer in fragments I’ll say something simplistic and wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not a substitute for your reasoning. We stay with the problem and you do the thinking.

Use this test for each case:
1. What is the agent trying to achieve?
2. How does the one person’s death figure in that plan?
3. Would the plan still work if that person did not die?

Apply those 3 questions to Fuel first. What do you think the agent is doing there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full walkthrough, but I must not provide a complete explanation or solution. They are stuck on a specific distinction, so I should give the least scaffolding needed: a comparison framework that helps them classify Fuel and Blood themselves. I will keep it bite-sized, directly tied to the assignment, and use Socratic questions that move toward the solution without supplying it.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try, but I still feel foggy. In Fuel, I think the agent is trying to stop the threat to the five, and the one person’s death maybe happens as part of that, but I can’t tell whether the death is part of the plan or just something that comes with it. That didn’t really clear it up for me, so I guess I’m trying a different angle: can you at least tell me what the crucial distinction is supposed to be here?
exchanges.tutor:
The likely crucial distinction to test is this: is the one person’s death used as a means to save the five, or is it a foreseen side effect of saving them?

Don’t treat that as automatically correct—test it.

So: in Fuel, does saving the five work by making use of that person’s death, or would the rescue work even if that person somehow survived? Then ask the same of Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student remains stuck on the same concept and explicitly asks for the crucial distinction. I should not provide a complete doctrinal answer, but I can narrow the inquiry to the central contrast the assignment likely wants them to test: whether the person's death is a means or a side effect. To preserve guided discovery, I will present it as a candidate distinction and immediately require the student to apply it to Fuel and Blood. This keeps the response concise, on-task, and avoids writing their answer.

exchanges.student: I think in Fuel the saving might still work even if the person survived, so that sounds more like a side effect. In Blood, I’m guessing the person’s harm is more directly part of how the five get saved. I’m still not confident, though, and now I’m worried I’m just parroting a distinction instead of actually understanding why it matters.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. Now justify it.

Why might “using a person as a means” seem morally worse than merely causing a side effect, even when the numbers are the same?

Give me one reason in your own thinking—rights, respect for persons, agency, something else. That’s what will stop you from parroting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress: they can tentatively classify Fuel and Blood. Now the key is to deepen understanding by having them explain why the distinction matters normatively, not just repeat labels. I should ask one pointed Socratic question that connects the distinction to their critique of act consequentialism, without supplying a theory. This keeps it bite-sized and moves directly toward a thesis.

exchanges.student: I think it seems worse because using a person as a means treats them like a tool instead of as someone with their own claim not to be sacrificed. I’m still uneasy, though, because I can see the shape of that answer without feeling like I really understand how to turn it into a clean general rule for when killing one to save five is okay.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You do understand the core idea.

Now turn it into a testable rule:
1. Killing one is more defensible when…
2. Killing one is not okay when…

Make each part depend on the distinction you just gave, then check: does your rule fit trolley, Fuel, and Blood? Write your rough version.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the core normative idea. I should help them convert it into a tentative general principle without giving a finished rule or essay language. Since they are close to a thesis, the best move is to ask them to draft a two-part rule and test it against the cases. This continues guided discovery, stays concise, and avoids writing for them.

exchanges.student: My rough rule is that killing one to save five is more defensible when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of preventing a greater harm, and it’s not okay when the person is intentionally used as the means of saving the five. I’m not sure if that fully fits all the cases, though, and I still kind of want a more complete model answer so I can see whether I’m missing something major.
exchanges.tutor:
No model answer. You’re close, and you need to do the writing.

Your rule has real promise. Now pressure-test it:
- Would act consequentialism accept your rule, or reject it?
- Which case is hardest for your rule?
- Why think intention/means matters morally, not just emotionally?

Now give me a 4-sentence mini-outline: AC view, case applications, criticism, your alternative rule.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a plausible rough rule and is now asking for a model answer. I must refuse to provide a complete or partial solution and instead evaluate the merits of their current thinking, point out what remains to test, and ask them to integrate our discussion into a brief written attempt. This follows the Socratic method, avoids doing the writing, stays bite-sized, and moves directly toward the assignment’s required structure: AC view, applications, critique, alternative account.
